---
title: "Map vs Object in JavaScript: A practical guide"
description: "Map vs Object in JavaScript: A practical guide"
draft: true
---

Have you ever wondered if you should choose a [Map](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Map) or plain old JavaScript Object?

Unless you're using features of Map that are impossible with objects (such as non-string keys),
the choice is not as trivial as one might think.

So I dug a bit deeper and decided to make a list of heuristic to help you choose the one that fits best.

> Note: This guide is strictly about performance of these data structures, not API choice.
> Your task may be better fitted for one or the other, or even perhaps
> a [Set](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Set) or
> a plain Array might suit you better. But sometimes, performance characteristics dictate design choices, too.

Before we proceed, here's another important note:

> This research is made in context of building UIs. While it's rare that you should need to care
about lookup performance, it's possible to have to sometimes deal with thousands and millions of units of data.
Whether those are data-heavy charts and visualizations or large data tables, you may have to deal with
situations involving high-frequency reads, writes, and object instantiations.

## General Considerations

Maps and Objects can outperform each other in different scenarios.

### Creating object is faster than creating maps

[Benchmark](https://jsbenchmark.com/#-IHJhbmRvbUludCgwLCBzaXplKSksXG4gIHN0cmluZ3M6IFsuLi5BcnJheShzaXplKS5rZXlzKCldLm1hcCgoKSA9PiByYW5kb21TdHJpbmcoKSksXG4gIG9iajoge30sXG4gIG1hcDogbmV3IE1hcCgpXG59In19)
